hero.title: Stop forgetting.
hero.desc: The SM-2 algorithm schedules your reviews — the same method used by polyglots and doctors. You flip the cards. The rest works on its own.

steps.title: Four steps to effective learning
steps.createSetDesc: Group your material by topic — a separate set for each subject, language, or chapter.
steps.addCardsDesc: Create them manually or paste text and let AI generate questions.

steps.rememberDesc: The SM-2 algorithm decides when to revisit a question. The same method used by polyglots learning 6+ languages.

demo.label: What does studying look like?
demo.title: Flip the card, rate yourself, move on
demo.subtitle: The SM-2 algorithm handles the rest. It decides when to revisit each question — so you don’t forget.
demo.question: Question
demo.questionText: Which protocol is responsible for reliable packet delivery in the TCP/IP model?
demo.answerText: TCP (Transmission Control Protocol) — provides flow control, retransmission, and segment ordering.

features.label: What you get
features.title: Methods backed by research
features.primaryDesc: Ebbinghaus proved that after 24 hours you remember only 30% of the material. The SM-2 algorithm — used by doctors, translators, and researchers — schedules reviews at increasing intervals. You review exactly when you’re about to forget.
features.pomodoroDesc: 25 minutes of study, 5 minutes of break. Zero distractions — just you and the material.
features.recallDesc: You don’t read notes. You recall. Research shows it works 3x better.
features.customDesc: Edit, create from scratch, delete. Or paste text and let AI help.
features.setsDesc: English, biology, driving test — each set is a separate knowledge pack.
features.cloudDesc: Laptop, phone, tablet. Your flashcards are waiting in the cloud.

learningGuide.title: Learning guide
learningGuide.subtitle: Discover proven, science-backed techniques for effective learning. Combine them with flashcards to learn faster and remember longer.
learningGuide.footerText: Ready to put these techniques into practice?

charts.spacedRepetitionImgAlt: Chart showing how reviews help maintain high retention of learned material
charts.activeRecallLabel: Effectiveness of study methods (% retained after one week):
charts.feynmanStep2Desc: As if explaining to a child
charts.feynmanStep3Desc: Go back to sources and fill the gaps

spacedRepetition.title: Spaced Repetition
spacedRepetition.shortDescription: A science-backed method of scheduling reviews that lets you remember more while studying less.
spacedRepetition.paragraph1: Spaced repetition is a learning technique where material is reviewed at progressively longer intervals. Instead of cramming everything at once, you spread your study sessions over time.
spacedRepetition.paragraph2: Scientific research confirms this is one of the most effective memorization strategies. The key is reviewing material just before you start forgetting it — the so-called "optimal review point."
spacedRepetition.paragraph3: The SM-2 algorithm, which powers many modern learning apps (including Memlo), automatically calculates the optimal moment for your next review based on how you rate the difficulty of the material.
spacedRepetition.paragraph4: Practical tip: study regularly, even if it's just 10–15 minutes a day. Consistency matters more than the length of any single session.
spacedRepetition.keyTakeaway: Regular, short review sessions are more effective than one-time, long study marathons.

activeRecall.title: Active Recall
activeRecall.shortDescription: Testing yourself instead of passively reading — the key to lasting memorization.
activeRecall.paragraph1: Active recall means actively retrieving information from memory instead of passively reviewing notes. When you try to answer a question without looking at the answer, your brain forms stronger neural connections.
activeRecall.paragraph2: Research shows that students who test themselves on material remember up to 50% more than those who merely re-read it. This is known as the "testing effect."
activeRecall.paragraph3: Flashcards are the perfect tool for active recall — you see the question (front of the card) and actively try to recall the answer before checking it.
activeRecall.paragraph4: Tip: don't get discouraged when you can't remember an answer. The effort of trying to recall strengthens memory, even when you fail to retrieve the information.
activeRecall.keyTakeaway: The effort you put into recalling information is what builds lasting memory.

forgettingCurve.title: Ebbinghaus Forgetting Curve
forgettingCurve.shortDescription: Why we forget and how reviews can flatten the forgetting curve.
forgettingCurve.paragraph1: In 1885, Hermann Ebbinghaus discovered that newly learned information is forgotten in a predictable way. Without reviews, we forget about 70% of material within 24 hours and over 90% within a week.
forgettingCurve.paragraph2: The forgetting curve shows an exponential decline in memory retention over time. However, each review "resets" the curve and slows the rate of forgetting — after several reviews, information enters long-term memory.
forgettingCurve.paragraph3: This discovery is the foundation of spaced repetition. The first review should happen the next day, the next after a few days, then after a week, a month, and so on.
forgettingCurve.paragraph4: That's why Memlo's algorithm schedules reviews at growing intervals — to catch you just before the moment of forgetting and reinforce the memory trace.
forgettingCurve.keyTakeaway: Without reviews you forget 70% within a day. Regular reviews can retain nearly 100% of knowledge.

dunningKruger.title: Dunning-Kruger Effect
dunningKruger.shortDescription: Why beginners overestimate their knowledge and experts underestimate it.
dunningKruger.paragraph1: The Dunning-Kruger effect is a psychological phenomenon where people with low competence in a given area overestimate their abilities, while highly competent people tend to underestimate theirs.
dunningKruger.paragraph2: In the context of learning, this means you may feel you "know everything" after reading the material once. This false sense of competence is dangerous — it leads to abandoning study too early.
dunningKruger.paragraph3: Recognizing this effect in yourself is the first step toward more effective learning. When you feel a topic is "easy," test yourself — you may be surprised how little you actually remember.
dunningKruger.paragraph4: Flashcards and active recall help break this effect because they force you to prove your knowledge instead of relying on a false sense of familiarity.
dunningKruger.keyTakeaway: If you feel it's "easy" — test yourself. True knowledge only reveals itself when you try to recall it.

feynmanTechnique.title: Feynman Technique
feynmanTechnique.shortDescription: Explain it simply — if you can't, you don't truly understand it yet.
feynmanTechnique.paragraph1: Richard Feynman, Nobel Prize-winning physicist, was known for explaining complex topics in simple terms. His learning technique is based on four steps.
feynmanTechnique.paragraph2: Step 1: Choose a topic and write down everything you know. Step 2: Explain it as if teaching a child — in simple language, no jargon. Step 3: Identify gaps in your explanation and return to source material. Step 4: Simplify and refine your explanation.
feynmanTechnique.paragraph3: This method is especially effective because it forces deep understanding rather than surface-level memorization. If you can't explain something in simple words, you don't understand it well enough.
feynmanTechnique.paragraph4: You can use flashcards for this — write a question on the front and your own simple explanation on the back, as plain as possible.
feynmanTechnique.keyTakeaway: True understanding is the ability to explain a topic in simple words.

pomodoro.title: Pomodoro Technique
pomodoro.shortDescription: Manage your study time in 25-minute blocks with breaks for recovery.
pomodoro.paragraph1: The Pomodoro Technique, developed by Francesco Cirillo in the 1980s, divides work into 25-minute blocks (called "pomodoros") separated by 5-minute breaks. After four blocks, you take a longer break (15–30 minutes).
pomodoro.paragraph2: Why does it work? Our brain is not designed for hours of uninterrupted focus. Short breaks prevent cognitive fatigue and maintain a high level of attention over a longer period.
pomodoro.paragraph3: Research shows that regular breaks not only improve focus but also support memory consolidation — the process by which the brain solidifies newly learned information.
pomodoro.paragraph4: Combine Pomodoro with flashcard study: one 25-minute block is ideal for a review session. Long enough to make progress, but not so long that you get tired.
pomodoro.keyTakeaway: 25 minutes of focused study + 5 minutes of break = more effective study time than hours-long marathons.

interleaving.title: Interleaving Topics
interleaving.shortDescription: Mixing different subjects during a single study session instead of focusing on just one.
interleaving.paragraph1: Interleaving is a strategy of alternating between different skills or topics within a single study session, instead of focusing on one subject at a time (called "blocking").
interleaving.paragraph2: Although interleaving may feel harder and less comfortable, research clearly shows it leads to better long-term retention and the ability to transfer knowledge to new situations.
interleaving.paragraph3: This happens because interleaving forces the brain to constantly "switch" between contexts, strengthening the ability to distinguish similar concepts and choose the right strategy.
interleaving.paragraph4: In practice: instead of reviewing flashcards from one deck for an hour, mix several decks from different topics. Your scores may be worse at first, but long-term retention will be significantly better.
interleaving.keyTakeaway: Mixing topics during study is harder, but leads to deeper and more lasting understanding.

growthMindset.title: Growth Mindset
growthMindset.shortDescription: Your brain is plastic — intelligence is not fixed, it can be developed.
growthMindset.paragraph1: Psychologist Carol Dweck identified two types of mindset: fixed ("I'm either talented or not") and growth ("I can develop through effort"). People with a growth mindset achieve significantly better learning outcomes.
growthMindset.paragraph2: Brain neuroplasticity confirms this scientifically — our brain physically changes and creates new neural connections every time we learn something. You literally become "smarter" with every study session.
growthMindset.paragraph3: The key is your approach to mistakes. When you don't remember an answer on a flashcard, don't treat it as failure — it's a signal that your brain needs another review, and each such review strengthens the memory trace.
growthMindset.paragraph4: Instead of saying "I can't learn this," say "I haven't learned this yet." This small shift in thinking has a huge impact on motivation and persistence.
growthMindset.keyTakeaway: Your intelligence is not fixed — every effort you put into learning physically strengthens your brain.

dkStages.stage1Label: I don't know what I don't know
dkStages.stage1Description: Beginner — high confidence, low competence. Typical after first contact with a topic.
dkStages.stage2Description: The Dunning-Kruger effect in full — "this is easy!" A false sense of competence.
dkStages.stage3Description: Realizing the scale of one's ignorance. The moment when many give up learning.
dkStages.stage4Description: Gradual growth of competence and confidence — this time based on real knowledge.
dkStages.stage5Description: Expertise — confidence comes from experience, but is lower than at the "peak of stupidity."
